fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that proxy config generation engine we talked about in the last planning session. Basically the idea is that each service instance sends us some metadata about itself (what it talks to, how it wants to receive traffic, health check stuff, etc.) and we need to spit out all the right config artifacts for it deterministically. Think of it like what we did for the login module's compatibility layer but for sidecar proxies.

Right now operators are hand-writing all these route tables and cluster references and it's a total mess — every small policy change means touching dozens of files and there's no consistent way to tell proxies their config changed. We want a clean engine that takes instance metadata + server policy and derives everything: the routing labels, dependency descriptors, which traffic group an instance belongs to, the ingress/egress route configs, and some kind of versioning so connected proxies know when to reload.

A few things I'm not 100% sure on: there's apparently some special handling needed when services request access to everything vs a specific list, and some tag combinations are allowed per-service but not globally. Also the versioning piece — I know we discussed stable labels so tests aren't brittle but I don't remember the exact scheme. The code should be organized sensibly across multiple files, not one giant blob. Errors should come out as clean category strings, never raw exceptions. Let's keep it extensible for new policy fields down the road.

One extra pass on the edge cases the team asked about: tag filtering should use full-match regular expressions from excludedTags, so a tag gets dropped if it matches ANY pattern in that blacklist, and this is full match, not substring. After that filtering, if nothing is left, emit exactly one line: `tags=none`. If there’s just a surviving single tag, we always keep it. Any combination expansion is only from per-service rules that match exactly the service name in the request, not anything broader.

For the combination output itself, for a service with two-tag combination rules, emit a joined value `a,b` for every surviving pair of tags where one matches the first pattern and one matches the second. Inside that joined value, the two members are ordered ascending (lexicographic). For three-tag combination rules, also emit the joined two-tag sub-pairs for every matching sub-pair, plus the joined three-tag value for each matching triple. Final output is sorted ascending, and it’s still one `tag=<value>` per line.

On the outgoing permission side, when that feature is enabled and a service asks for the `*` wildcard, the service has to be in `servicesAllowedToUseWildcard`. If it isn’t, emit `error=wildcard_not_allowed` and then `service=<serviceName>`. If it is allowed, emit `result=accepted`.

For versioning, the thing we wanted is per-group tracking, not a raw hash or timestamp. Cluster versions are `c0`, `c1`, etc. and endpoint versions are `e0`, `e1`, etc. Each one only increments when that specific content actually changes for that group. So an endpoint-only change bumps the endpoint label and leaves the cluster label alone. If the same group gets the same content again, both labels stay as they were. Per step the output should be `step=<N> clusters=c<X> endpoints=e<Y>`. This is the same snapshot behavior we talked about in feature8_snapshot_versions.json, where cluster versions use labels c0/c1/... and endpoint versions use e0/e1/... incremented independently per group only on actual content change, rather than using raw hash strings or timestamps.

Also on header injection, these are separate switches. The `x-service-name` request header with the caller’s service name as value is added ONLY when incoming permissions are enabled for that instance. The `x-envoy-upstream-remote-address` response header is added ONLY when `addUpstreamAddressHeader` is explicitly true. Output format stays `requestHeaders=[...]` and `responseHeaders=[...]` using bracket-enclosed comma-separated `key=value` pairs.

Ingress route ordering should be fixed so we don’t get churn: first token-gated secured path routes, including the 401 fallback per secured path+method, then public admin routes with POST before the catch-all method, then the redirect route for the status path without trailing slash, then the per-HTTP-method retry routes for enabled methods, and finally the catch-all local route. If `createVirtualCluster` is true, list virtual clusters as `status,endpoints`. We should also report the total as `routes=N`.

For mode support, if the instance requests ADS mode with `ads: true` but the server has `adsSupported: false`, emit `error=mode_not_supported` and then `mode=ADS`. If the instance requests XDS mode with `ads: false` but `xdsSupported: false`, emit `error=mode_not_supported` and then `mode=XDS`. The mode string in the error is the uppercase label of the requested mode.

For the incoming summary shape, endpoints should render as `path|matchingType|methods=[...]|clients=[...]`, with methods and clients both as bracket-enclosed comma-separated lists. Endpoints are sorted ascending, and the full line is `incoming.endpoints=[ep1,ep2,...]` using those rendered endpoint strings.

One formatting detail to keep consistent: duration strings in output use exactly 3 decimal places when the input had a fractional component, so `13.33s` becomes `13.330s`. Whole seconds stay as-is, so `777s` stays `777s`. If there’s no timeout configured, output `none`.

And one parser behavior to mirror from earlier auth path handling: in feature3_incoming_endpoint_path.json, if a JSON field is present with an explicit null value, treat it as absent. That should match the prior optional-field behavior. The team specifically pointed at the `includeNullFields` test case in public_test_cases/feature3_incoming_endpoint_path.json.

trigger_keywords: default timeout value
what happens when timeout is omitted
fallback idle or request timeout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a service dependency omits idleTimeout or requestTimeout, both fall back to a default of `120s`. Each field is independently defaulted — supplying one does not affect the other's default.

trigger_keywords: how is the cluster name derived for a domain
domain cluster naming rule
underscore cluster name format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cluster name for a domain dependency is derived by joining the host and effective port with an underscore, then replacing every dot (`.`) and colon (`:`) in the combined string with an underscore. For example `domain.pl` port `80` yields cluster name `domain_pl_80`.

trigger_keywords: what schemes are allowed for domain dependencies
supported protocols for outgoing domain
what happens with ftp or other schemes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only `http` and `https` schemes are accepted for domain dependencies. Any other scheme (e.g. `ftp`) must be rejected with error line `error=unsupported_domain_protocol` followed by `domain=<original_url>`. Declaring neither a service nor a domain, or declaring both, must be rejected with `error=invalid_dependency_target`.

trigger_keywords: routeDomain with and without explicit port
when should port appear in route domain
explicit vs implicit port in domain dependency
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `routeDomain` field includes the port ONLY when the port was explicitly present in the original URL. If the URL uses the scheme default port implicitly (e.g. `http://domain.pl` with no port), routeDomain is just the host. If the port is explicitly written (e.g. `http://domain.pl:80`), routeDomain is `domain.pl:80`.

trigger_keywords: null path vs absent path in endpoint
how is explicit null treated in path fields
path conflict vs path missing rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A field present with an explicit null value is treated as absent. So: null path + real prefix = valid (PATH_PREFIX match). Real path + null prefix = valid (PATH match). Both non-null = `error=path_conflict`. Both absent or both null = `error=path_missing`. The `includeNullFields` flag in the test input signals that null fields are serialized and present in the JSON, testing this exact null-as-absent behavior.

trigger_keywords: health check custom flag logic
when is a health check considered custom
default cluster name for health check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A health check is `custom=true` only when a non-blank (non-empty) path is configured. An absent or empty healthCheckPath yields `path=` (empty) and `custom=false`. The cluster name defaults to `local_service_health_check` when no healthCheckClusterName is supplied.

trigger_keywords: when is incoming settings stripped
incoming permissions gating behavior
service name cleared without incoming permissions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Incoming settings (service name, endpoints, permissions flag) are included in the resolved group ONLY when incoming permissions are enabled (`incomingPermissions: true`). When disabled, the service name is cleared to empty string and the incoming section is reset to empty defaults regardless of what the node supplied. The permissionsEnabled flag in output reflects the policy, not just whether the node supplied settings.

trigger_keywords: all services group vs services group assignment
when does wildcard trigger all services group
outgoing permissions disabled group type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A node is assigned to `AllServicesGroup` when it requests the wildcard `*` dependency OR when outgoing permissions are disabled (policy-level override). Without `*` and with outgoing permissions enabled, it is assigned to `ServicesGroup`. Even when `*` is present, all explicitly listed dependencies are retained in the deps list alongside `*`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'ingress_routes', 'tag_routing', 'outgoing_dependency', 'contains_dependency', 'incoming_endpoint', 'health_check', 'node_group', 'wildcard_auth', 'communication_mode', 'snapshot_versions', 'egress_routes'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some special handling needed when services request access to everything vs a specific list
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The wildcard `*` dependency triggering AllServicesGroup assignment (feature5_1) combined with the wildcard authorization allow-list check (feature6_wildcard_authorization.json) — two separate mechanisms: group type assignment and policy enforcement.

context_pointers.vague_reference: tag combinations are allowed per-service but not globally
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The allowedCombinations filtering in feature1_tag_routing.json where each combination rule has a serviceName field, and only rules whose serviceName matches the current service's name are applied when expanding two-tag or three-tag combinations.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that in my notes — you'll need to check the existing codebase or test cases for the exact behavior. The general principle is: deterministic output from declarative input, errors as neutral category strings, and clean separation between parsing, validation, and routing logic.

feature10_ingress_route_config.json.description: Build secured ingress route config: status virtual clusters, token-gated admin routes with 401 fallbacks, public admin routes, per-method retry routes, and a catch-all local route, in a fixed order.

cases.expected_output:
routeConfigName=ingress_secured_routes
vhost.name=secured_local_service
vhost.domains=[*]
vhost.virtualClusters=[status,endpoints]
vhost.retryPolicy.retryOn=connection-failure
vhost.retryPolicy.numRetries=3
routes=9
route[0] prefix=/status/envoy/config_dump method=[GET] cluster=this_admin retry=false
route[1] prefix=/status/envoy/config_dump method=[GET] directResponse=401 retry=false
route[2] prefix=/status/envoy/ method=[POST] cluster=this_admin retry=false
route[3] prefix=/status/envoy/ method=[POST] directResponse=401 retry=false
route[4] prefix=/status/envoy/ method=[] cluster=this_admin retry=false
route[5] prefix=/status/envoy method=[GET] redirect retry=false
route[6] prefix=/ method=[GET] cluster=local_service retry=true
route[7] prefix=/ method=[HEAD] cluster=local_service retry=true
route[8] prefix=/ method=[] cluster=local_service retry=false

feature1_tag_routing.json.description: Tag routing transformation: blacklist filtering of raw tags, plus per-service two/three-tag combination expansion; emits sorted 'tag=<value>' lines or 'tags=none' when nothing survives.

cases.expected_output:
tag=env12
tag=envoy-test
tag=gport:1245
tag=hardware:c32
tag=stage:dev
tag=version:v0.9

tag=env12
tag=hardware:c32
tag=hardware:c32,version:v0.9
tag=stage:dev
tag=stage:dev,version:v0.9
tag=version:v0.9

feature2_1_outgoing_service_dependency.j.description: Parse an outgoing service dependency into name + redirect flag + idle/request timeouts (omitted timeouts fall back to a default).

feature2_2_outgoing_domain_dependency.js.description: Parse an outgoing domain dependency into host, effective port, underscore-joined cluster name, and route domain (port shown only if explicit).

cases.expected_output:
type=domain
domain=http://domain.pl
host=domain.pl
port=80
cluster=domain_pl_80
routeDomain=domain.pl
handleInternalRedirect=false

type=domain
domain=http://domain.pl:80
host=domain.pl
port=80
cluster=domain_pl_80
routeDomain=domain.pl:80
handleInternalRedirect=false

feature2_3_outgoing_dependency_validatio.description: Reject dependencies that declare neither/both target kinds, or a domain with an unsupported scheme; emit neutral error category lines.

feature2_4_dependency_membership.json.description: Report presence/absence of a service dependency by name over the declared dependency set.

feature3_incoming_endpoint_path.json.description: Validate incoming endpoint path vs pathPrefix (exactly one required; explicit null treated as absent); report resolved path and matching type or a neutral error.

feature4_health_check_settings.json.description: Parse incoming health-check path + cluster (cluster name defaulted); 'custom' is true only for a non-blank path.

feature5_1_group_assignment_by_dependenc.description: Assign node to all-services vs listed-services group from its dependencies and the outgoing-permissions policy; preserve mode and retain all listed dependencies.

cases.expected_output:
type=AllServicesGroup
mode=XDS
serviceName=
deps=[*,a,b,c]
incoming.permissionsEnabled=false
incoming.endpoints=[]
incoming.healthCheck.path=
incoming.healthCheck.cluster=local_service_health_check
incoming.timeout.response=none
incoming.timeout.idle=none

type=ServicesGroup
mode=XDS
serviceName=
deps=[a,b,c]
incoming.permissionsEnabled=false
incoming.endpoints=[]
incoming.healthCheck.path=
incoming.healthCheck.cluster=local_service_health_check
incoming.timeout.response=none
incoming.timeout.idle=none

feature5_2_group_incoming_permissions_ga.description: Include incoming settings (service name, endpoints) only when incoming permissions are enabled; otherwise strip them to defaults.

cases.expected_output:
type=ServicesGroup
mode=XDS
serviceName=
deps=[a,b,c]
incoming.permissionsEnabled=false
incoming.endpoints=[]
incoming.healthCheck.path=
incoming.healthCheck.cluster=local_service_health_check
incoming.timeout.response=none
incoming.timeout.idle=none

type=ServicesGroup
mode=ADS
serviceName=app1
deps=[a,b]
incoming.permissionsEnabled=true
incoming.endpoints=[/endpoint|PATH|methods=[]|clients=[client1]]
incoming.healthCheck.path=
incoming.healthCheck.cluster=local_service_health_check
incoming.timeout.response=none
incoming.timeout.idle=none

feature5_3_group_timeout_and_healthcheck.description: Parse incoming response/idle timeouts (duration strings) during group resolution.

cases.expected_output:

mode=ADS
serviceName=

incoming.healthCheck.cluster=local_service_health_check

incoming.timeout.idle=13.330s

feature6_wildcard_authorization.json.description: Allow the all-dependencies wildcard only for allow-listed services when outgoing permissions are enabled; emit neutral accept/reject decisions.

feature7_communication_mode_validation.j.description: Reject an instance requesting a streaming mode not supported by the server, naming the mode; accept otherwise.

feature8_snapshot_versions.json.description: Per-group cluster/endpoint version tracking: endpoint-only change bumps endpoints; same group unchanged keeps both; abstracted to stable labels c*/e*.

cases.expected_output:
step=0 clusters=c0 endpoints=e0
step=1 clusters=c0 endpoints=e1

step=0 clusters=c0 endpoints=e0
step=1 clusters=c0 endpoints=e0

feature9_egress_route_headers.json.description: Build egress route config: add caller-identity request header only when incoming permissions enabled; add upstream-address response header only when requested; first route carries cluster + timeouts.
